mappings.rationaleAI: Directly addresses population composition, fertility, mortality, migration, demographic transition, and life expectancy (outline A).
Directly addresses Malthusian population theory and its influence on subsequent demographic thought (outline A.3.d).
Governmental policies and theories influencing population composition -- including heredity and population control -- are central to outline A.4.
Migration as a determinant of population composition and change is relevant to outline A.1.c.
Refugee movements as a form of forced migration affect population distribution (outline A.1.c).
Population movements and the formation of diaspora communities affect demographic composition (outline A.1.c).
Mortality rates, disease patterns, and public health factors affecting population are central to outline A.1.b.
Public health infrastructure affecting mortality, longevity, and population health in communities connects to outline A.1.b.
Pandemics as events affecting mortality rates and historical population changes connect to outline A.1.b and A.2.
Directly addresses urban planning methods, social aspects of redevelopment, and regional planning (outline B.3).
Suburbanization, sprawl, and the growth of residential communities surrounding cities connect to outline B.4.b.
Urban segregation and its social dimensions are relevant to social aspects of urban planning (outline B.3.b).
The built environment of cities is relevant to methods and materials of urban planning (outline B.3.a).
Modern architectural approaches to urban design and city planning connect to outline B.3.a.
Landscape design in urban planning and the relationship between cities and their environments are relevant to outline B.3.
Historical forces driving urbanization and the growth of industrial cities shaped outline B.2.

mappings.vsiAuthor: John A. Matthews, David T. Herbert
mappings.rationaleAI: Spatial distribution of populations, interaction between urban and rural communities, and physical geography connect to outline B.4.c.
Migration, inequality, and spatial dimensions of human populations in urban and rural settings are relevant to outline A and B.
Environmental and climatic change as problems affecting urban growth and population connect to outline B.4.f.
Political responses to pollution, environmental degradation, and population-environment interactions connect to outline B.4.e and B.4.f.
Economic dimensions of pollution control and natural resource conservation are relevant to population growth (outline A.3.g and B.4.f).
Ethical dimensions of the relationship between population growth and environmental conservation connect to outline A.3.g.
Sustainable development in relation to population growth and urban environmental challenges is relevant to outline A.3.g and B.4.e.
Ecological theories illuminate the relationship between human population growth and natural resource conservation (outline A.3.g).
The impact of urbanization and population growth on the environment -- including land use change and pollution -- connects to outline B.4.f.
Global economic forces driving urbanization, mass migration, and megalopolis formation are relevant to outline B.4.a.
Regional integration and the relationship between population, territory, and political organization connect to outline B.4.c.
Sociological perspectives on urbanization, community structure, and social effects of population change connect to outline B.3.b.
Borders affect migration patterns and population distribution across regions (outline A.1.c and B.4.c).
Future population projections, migration trends, and ecological scenarios are relevant to population outlook (outline A.5).
